title: Full Disclosure

jokes.text: Radio hosts discussing the VP fired a secret service man for smiling; correction that he was 'reassigned' as if that changes anything
jokes.setup: Radio hosts report the VP fired a secret service man for smiling, which sounds absurd
jokes.punchline: The correction that he was merely 'reassigned' is treated as a meaningful distinction but underscores how petty the original act was
jokes.explanation: A solid cold-open comedic framing device. The split-second correction makes the whole situation more ridiculous rather than less. Functional and efficient exposition-as-comedy.

jokes.text: 'I have a black belt in Mike Kwan Do.'
jokes.setup: Mike says he can 'bust some moves in this town' — already a cringeworthy boast
jokes.punchline: He invents a martial art named after himself to back it up
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect little joke. 'Mike Kwan Do' is a coined word that captures Mike's delusional self-regard in four syllables. It escalates his 'bust some moves' boast in a perfectly absurd way that is uniquely character-specific — no other character in this show could land this joke. High quotability. Nearly earns an 8 but is too slight a moment to reach that tier.

jokes.text: Amy's flat 'All right, sir, no.' in response to Mike Kwan Do
jokes.setup: Mike has just declared himself a black belt in 'Mike Kwan Do'
jokes.punchline: Amy's total, exhausted refusal in three words is funnier than any actual counter-argument
jokes.explanation: Amy's straight-woman exhaustion is a recurring comedic engine. 'All right, sir, no.' is maximally economical — three words covering dismissal, hierarchy acknowledgment, and utter exasperation. The 'sir' is the killer detail.

jokes.text: 'Hey, hey, easy, dough boy. Hands off the suit.'
jokes.setup: Amy presumably touches or grabs Mike physically to stop him from dancing/boasting
jokes.punchline: Mike calls her 'dough boy' — a fat joke — while protesting his suit being touched, a wildly disproportionate response
jokes.explanation: A serviceable insult that's a bit lazy — 'dough boy' is generic. The humor mostly comes from Mike's obliviousness and misplaced vanity about his suit, which is character-consistent but not sharp writing.

jokes.text: Dan on phone: 'Oh, I take no pleasure in my colleague's very public, brutal...' — then interrupted by Jonah telling him to move away from the TV
jokes.setup: Dan is performing false sympathy for Amy's humiliation on the phone
jokes.punchline: He's so flagrantly lying that he can't even finish the sentence before being physically interrupted by Jonah, who has no interest in his performance
jokes.explanation: Dan's performative fake-sympathy is a consistent character beat and the interruption cuts it perfectly. The joke is in the architecture: Dan crafting a show of magnanimity while being cut off by someone who doesn't care.

jokes.text: 'You must have a huge schadenboner over this Amy flack.'
jokes.setup: Dan is pretending not to enjoy Amy's humiliation
jokes.punchline: Jonah coins 'schadenboner' — a portmanteau of schadenfreude and erection — cutting right through the pretense
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout coined words. 'Schadenboner' is genuinely brilliant — it does exactly what great portmanteau insults should do: compress a complex psychological observation (taking sexual pleasure in another's downfall) into a single vulgar, precise, and memorable word. Highly quotable, instantly understood, and characteristically crude in the way Veep does best. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'God, she is gonna go ape-shit menstrual over this.' / 'Hey, I'm on the phone with AT&T. Can you stop shouting "menstrual"?'
jokes.setup: Dan uses the phrase 'ape-shit menstrual' — already an absurdly vivid compound insult
jokes.punchline: Someone nearby (Jonah/Dan's roommate) is on the phone with AT&T customer service and objects specifically to the word 'menstrual' being shouted
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is clean: absurd insult → mundane domestic interruption → the specific objection to 'menstrual' rather than the whole phrase is what makes it land. The AT&T detail grounds the absurdity in recognizable reality.

jokes.text: Dan's extended metaphor: 'broken fucking condom' pregnancy rumor / 'dead Dan walk' — punctuated by the sound of something breaking in the apartment
jokes.setup: Dan is listing all the reasons Jonah is more screwed than Amy — building an elaborate metaphorical case
jokes.punchline: He reaches his grand conclusion ('dead Dan walk') just as something physically breaks in the apartment, deflating his rhetorical momentum completely
jokes.explanation: The comedic deflation of Dan's speechifying by a mundane domestic accident is a solid anti-climax gag. Dan's rhetorical pomposity interrupted by something breaking mid-crescendo is structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: 'This is going in with the dirty dishes, man. For fuck's sake. If this is broken, I'm adding it to your fucking rent.'
jokes.setup: Something has broken in the apartment — implicitly a shared item between Dan and Jonah
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Dan and Jonah are roommates, implied by the rent threat — two political rivals sharing domestic life
jokes.explanation: The joke isn't in the line itself but in the world-building reveal: Dan and Jonah are roommates. This recontextualizes their entire dynamic — sworn political frenemies forced into domestic cohabitation. The banality of 'I'm adding it to your rent' underscores the absurdity magnificently.

jokes.text: 'I was gonna say that brings a smile to my face, but that would be safe, wouldn't it, ma'am?' — police official making a smiling joke to Selina
jokes.setup: Selina is famous today for firing someone for smiling; the police official is wrapping up a meeting
jokes.punchline: He almost says 'brings a smile to my face' — catches himself — makes the meta-joke about not smiling being 'safe' in her presence
jokes.explanation: A competent in-world joke that the episode's premise enables. The police chief's self-aware humor is a nice touch — someone from outside the VP bubble commenting on the absurdity. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'That's very funny, Bill. You're fired. I'm kidding. I'm kidding, I'm kidding.'
jokes.setup: Bill has just made a light joke about not smiling
jokes.punchline: Selina reflexively says 'you're fired' — the exact thing she's in trouble for — then has to rapidly walk it back
jokes.explanation: Selina's self-defeating behavior is the core of her character — she literally re-enacts the thing she's in trouble for. The triple 'I'm kidding' is perfect escalating panic. Strong character integration: only Selina would be this obliviously self-sabotaging.

jokes.text: 'Hey, ma'am, it's leave o'clock.'
jokes.setup: Selina has been in the meeting too long; Gary needs to signal departure
jokes.punchline: 'Leave o'clock' — Gary's infantilizing, overly cute way of telling the VP it's time to go, as if she were a child
jokes.explanation: Gary's infantilizing coddling of Selina is a running character comedy engine, and 'leave o'clock' perfectly encapsulates his obsequious, baby-talk relationship to her. High character integration score — this is utterly Gary.

jokes.text: 'Secret' as in shut the fuck up. And 'service' as in you work for me, okay? So why don't you shut the fuck up?
jokes.setup: Selina is raging about the secret service leak
jokes.punchline: She does a pseudo-dictionary breakdown of 'Secret Service' that reduces to 'shut the fuck up' twice — the second leg weaker than the first
jokes.explanation: A solid Selina rage-speech with a neat rhetorical structure — she deconstructs the phrase to extract her preferred meaning. The second leg ('service as in you work for me') is a slight letdown after the first, but the overall delivery and structure work.

jokes.text: 'We have an enemy and I want a name and a severed head that answers to that name, or would if it could still talk.'
jokes.setup: Selina demands accountability for the leak
jokes.punchline: The escalating demand for a severed head — then the logical self-correction that it couldn't answer if severed — which she includes anyway
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The joke has a three-part structure: (1) hyperbolic medieval demand for a severed head, (2) the bizarrely literal qualification 'that answers to that name,' (3) the self-undermining correction 'or would if it could still talk.' The self-aware absurdist logic — she realizes mid-sentence the severed head can't answer and just... keeps going — is what elevates this from a standard rage-hyperbole to something genuinely brilliant. Quotable, character-perfect, and structurally elegant. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'Ooh, Gary, did the veep just bitch-slap you with a door?'
jokes.setup: Selina storms into her office and presumably slams the door, hitting Gary who was too close
jokes.punchline: A bystander narrates the physical indignity with a cheerful 'ooh' — the visual gag of Gary being door-slapped by the woman he adores
jokes.explanation: A physical comedy beat that relies on the visual — Gary being literally hit by Selina's door is a perfect metaphor for his relationship with her. The narrator's cheerful 'ooh' is the right comedic tone. Works well as a transitional beat.

jokes.text: Gary: 'She didn't mean that. She's got a lot on her mind today.' / Staffer: 'Oh, yeah, you mean like firing Amy? Or someone who's undermining the president...'
jokes.setup: Gary reflexively defends Selina after she literally hits him with a door
jokes.punchline: His defense is met with a list of all the things on Selina's mind — none of which Gary acknowledges because his loyalty is absolute and blind
jokes.explanation: Gary's Stockholm-syndrome loyalty to Selina is consistently funny. The joke is in the gap between Gary's unconditional defense and the obvious reality everyone else sees. Solid character work.

jokes.text: 'Oh, what, is she out of hand sanitizer again?'
jokes.setup: Gary says he needs to tell the team something important
jokes.punchline: The staffer guesses Selina's 'crisis' is running out of hand sanitizer — implying this is a thing that has happened
jokes.explanation: A quick character detail joke that does a lot with a little — it implies a whole backstory of Selina's neurotic high-maintenance demands treated as office crises. Economy is the joke's strength.

jokes.text: 'Gary, why are you talking in cliffhangers?'
jokes.setup: Gary is dramatically building up to something, clearly struggling to say it
jokes.punchline: Amy cuts through his drama with a perfectly precise meta-observation about his speech pattern
jokes.explanation: 'Talking in cliffhangers' is a sharp, quotable observation that perfectly captures Gary's dramatic, enabling style. Amy's deadpan frustration is her comic signature and this is one of her better lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Gary reveals: 'Selina's had a miscarriage.' — followed immediately by Dan: 'Well, this is good for us.'
jokes.setup: Gary has dramatically revealed the VP's deeply personal miscarriage to the staff
jokes.punchline: Dan's instant, unsentimental political calculation: 'Well, this is good for us.'
jokes.explanation: The best pure beat in the episode. The structural genius is in the timing: Gary delivers the genuine emotional gut-punch of a miscarriage, the expected beat is a moment of human response, and Dan immediately converts it to political calculus with four words. 'Well, this is good for us' is both funny and character-defining — it tells you everything about Dan in one sentence. The 'nicely done, Dandroid' follow-up confirms everyone clocked the darkness. The pivot speed is what creates the laugh: zero processing time, zero humanity. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'How is she?' / 'Free from a major fucking political headache, I'd say.'
jokes.setup: Amy asks the humane question — how is Selina after her miscarriage?
jokes.punchline: Dan answers with a purely political assessment, not even acknowledging the emotional register of Amy's question
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback and escalation of joke 19. Dan doubling down on the political-only framing when directly asked a human question is the comedy of his sociopathy. 'Free from a major fucking political headache' is the exact wrong answer delivered with total confidence. Strong craft and impact — Dan's character at its darkest and funniest.

jokes.text: 'That's deep space cold, Dan. Explains why you piss liquid nitrogen.'
jokes.setup: Someone calls Dan's response 'deep space cold'
jokes.punchline: The follow-up line escalates to the physical: he pisses liquid nitrogen — a vivid, absurdist articulation of Dan's coldness
jokes.explanation: 'Piss liquid nitrogen' is a strong insult that extends the 'deep space cold' metaphor with a viscerally funny physical image. The escalation from abstract (deep space) to bodily function is good comedy craft. Quotable.

jokes.text: Gary, having just revealed the VP's miscarriage to the room, pivots to pretend he was telling them about his riding mower
jokes.setup: A colleague walks in and asks what's going on mid-conversation about the miscarriage
jokes.punchline: Gary instantly invents 'I was telling the guys about my riding mower I got' — the most mundane possible cover story
jokes.explanation: The comedic gap between 'Vice President had a miscarriage' and 'riding mower' is enormous, and the specificity of the riding mower (not just 'a mower') makes it funnier. Gary's complete non-sequitur competence at damage control is a strong character beat.

jokes.text: 'Wow, Gary. That was quick. Look at that.'
jokes.setup: Gary has just implausibly pivoted from the miscarriage to riding mowers
jokes.punchline: Amy's flat acknowledgment — not impressed, not amused, just clinically noting the speed of the lie
jokes.explanation: Serviceable reaction beat that punctuates joke 22. Amy's flat delivery is reliable but this particular line doesn't add much beyond acknowledging the previous gag.

jokes.text: 'White House is in the house. * Everybody say way-o! *' — presumably Jonah singing/announcing his own entrance
jokes.setup: The White House liaison enters the VP's office
jokes.punchline: Jonah announces himself with a hip-hop/call-and-response entrance, completely unaware of or unbothered by how embarrassing this is
jokes.explanation: Pure Jonah — oblivious, self-aggrandizing, and mortifying. His self-announced entrance with a call-and-response he expects others to join perfectly captures his delusion. 'Everybody say way-o' landing into silence is a cringe comedy beat that the show does well.

jokes.text: 'The skyscraper of shit has arrived.'
jokes.setup: Jonah enters with his embarrassing announcement
jokes.punchline: Amy's sotto voce description: not just a pile of shit but a 'skyscraper' of it — architecture-scale contempt
jokes.explanation: 'Skyscraper of shit' is a good Veep insult — it has the right scale and absurdist specificity. The architectural upgrade from the standard 'pile of shit' is what makes it. Quotable, efficient, character-perfect from Amy.

jokes.text: The viral video tagged 'Selina Sell-Outta' — a compilation of her contradictions including firing someone for smiling
jokes.setup: Jonah shows Selina a viral video about her
jokes.punchline: The video cuts together her contradictions with pointed editorial framing — 'Selina Sell-Outta' as the tag
jokes.explanation: The video-within-show is a solid satirical device — the show doing political satire within the show's political satire. 'Sell-Outta' is a decent pun. The extended clip creates cringe discomfort for Selina that drives the scene forward.

jokes.text: 'I am Selina Meyer. And I don't have any idea what's going on in my office.'
jokes.setup: The viral video has been editorializing about Selina's contradictions
jokes.punchline: The video cuts to Selina directly saying she doesn't know what's going on in her own office — the most damning clip possible, played for maximum irony
jokes.explanation: The joke operates on multiple levels: it's funny as a clip, it's funnier because Selina is watching it, and it's funniest because it's true. Selina watching herself confess her incompetence in a viral video while her incompetence is actively unfolding is strong structural comedy.

jokes.text: 'I love this part.' — Jonah's reaction while watching the video
jokes.setup: The viral video is clearly humiliating for Selina; she's watching it surrounded by her staff
jokes.punchline: Jonah cheerfully announces his enjoyment — in her presence — while she watches
jokes.explanation: Jonah's social obliviousness is his comedic engine, and 'I love this part' while standing next to the person being humiliated is a clean expression of it. High character integration, economical delivery.

jokes.text: 'All right, pack up your limbs and get the fuck out, Jonah.' / 'Is punching allowed?' / 'Oh, you wouldn't stand a chance. I have a much longer reach.'
jokes.setup: Selina throws Jonah out; someone asks if punching is allowed
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response isn't 'no' or 'please don't' — it's a confident assessment of his physical advantage ('I have a much longer reach'), as if accepting the challenge
jokes.explanation: Jonah's physical self-confidence is funny because it's simultaneously probably true (he is very tall) and completely wrong-headed — he treats a potential assault as a competition he can win. 'Pack up your limbs' is also a nice Selina insult.

jokes.text: 'You are the closest to normal we've got going in this office right now.' / 'Thanks, I think.'
jokes.setup: Selina assigns Mike to talk to the reassigned secret service agent as the most normal person in the office
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'thanks, I think' — the correct response to a backhanded compliment that implies everyone else is abnormal
jokes.explanation: A solid ironic compliment exchange. The joke is in the institutional dysfunction it implies — Mike is the baseline for normal in this room. His uncertainty about whether to be flattered is exactly right.

jokes.text: The Full Disclosure scheme: 'by showing that we have nothing to hide, then we can actually hide some stuff.'
jokes.setup: The team is panicking about releasing records; Selina proposes releasing everything
jokes.punchline: Her explicit statement of the cynical logic — transparency as cover for opacity — delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: This is strong political satire with real comedic craft. Selina articulates a real political tactic (information overload as concealment) out loud in the most brazenly cynical possible way. It's funny as a joke and sharp as satire. 'You can't read everything. I don't read half the stuff I'm supposed to' is a perfect supporting beat. Quotable and rewatch-worthy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Oh, it's fine. I mean, it was like a heavy period. Don't worry about it.'
jokes.setup: Someone has accidentally referenced 'baby' in front of Selina after her miscarriage; everyone is mortified
jokes.punchline: Selina waves it off with startling clinical casualness — 'like a heavy period' — to end the awkward moment, immediately returning to business
jokes.explanation: Selina's self-protective deflection of genuine personal pain with a blunt, clinical, slightly shocking phrase is one of the most character-specific jokes in the episode. Only Selina — a politician who has subordinated everything personal to the political — would minimize her own miscarriage to keep a meeting moving. 'Like a heavy period' is the exact phrase that makes this land: too specific, too clinical, too casual. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: The 'partial disclosure light' scene — Selina immediately explains they will NOT be doing full disclosure
jokes.setup: Selina has just dramatically announced 'Full disclosure is now the name of the game'
jokes.punchline: The very next beat (different room, same meeting presumably): 'All right, obviously it is not gonna be full disclosure. Okay? It's gonna be partial disclosure light.'
jokes.explanation: The instant reversal of the full disclosure declaration is a perfect Veep beat: the gap between public posture and private reality is the whole show. 'Partial disclosure light' is a great phrase — it's got the absurd corporate modifier ('light') and the frank admission of limitation.

jokes.text: 'There can be no... Are you writing that down? Why would you be writing that down?'
jokes.setup: Selina is listing things to hide from the disclosure; a staffer apparently takes notes
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of someone writing down the list of things they're supposed to not have written down
jokes.explanation: The institutional incompetence joke — someone is actively creating a paper trail of the things they're trying to hide. The meta-absurdity of the cover-up undoing itself in real time is good Veep material.

jokes.text: 'We have to check Sue's calendar, make sure there are no meetings there that I did-didn't have.'
jokes.setup: Selina is listing things to scrub from the disclosure
jokes.punchline: Her self-correction mid-sentence reveals the cover: she's looking for meetings that she DID have but needs to pretend she DIDN'T — the stutter exposing the deception
jokes.explanation: The Freudian slip mid-sentence is a nice subtle joke — Selina almost says the true thing before correcting herself. The stutter ('did-didn't') captures politicians' practiced self-censorship failing in private.

jokes.text: 'The accidentally racist brochure that we had to shred.'
jokes.setup: Selina is listing things to redact from the disclosure
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of 'the accidentally racist brochure' as if it's a routine administrative matter
jokes.explanation: Six words that imply an entire catastrophic backstory: someone in this office produced and shredded a racist brochure, and the most notable thing about it is the modifier 'accidentally.' The economy is superb — it's a whole episode's worth of scandal compressed into a throwaway. Very quotable and very Veep.

jokes.text: 'So it doesn't look as if we've just airbrushed the nipples out of this fucking thing.'
jokes.setup: Selina wants something genuinely embarrassing in the disclosure so the scrubbing isn't obvious
jokes.punchline: Her metaphor for sanitizing the disclosure: airbrushing nipples — specific, vivid, absurd, and slightly disturbing in context
jokes.explanation: The 'airbrushed nipples' metaphor is characteristically Selina — vivid, unexpected, slightly vulgar, and actually precise. It communicates the exact idea (visible sanitization is worse than no transparency) in an arresting way.

jokes.text: Selina's convoluted non-denial about Macauley: 'I don't know what you did or didn't do, but I do know that I can't know what you know or you don't know. You know?'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to give Dan plausible deniability (and herself) about the Macauley amendment dealings
jokes.punchline: The sentence becomes a recursive knot of 'know/don't know' that ends with 'you know?' — asking if he understands her incomprehensible statement
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in political language parody. Selina's tortured attempt to establish mutual deniability collapses into a grammatical oroboros that's genuinely hard to parse — and then she asks 'you know?' as if it were perfectly clear. It's both a joke about political doublespeak and a character joke about Selina's self-deception. 'I do know that I can't know what you know or you don't know' is brilliant in its recursive meaninglessness. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'I mean, I don't know what you did or didn't do, but I do know that I can't know what you know or you don't know.' — Dan's reply: 'I know.'
jokes.setup: Selina has delivered her incomprehensible 'know/don't know' speech
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'I know' — which is both the linguistically correct response to 'you know?' and an absurd claim that he understood any of it
jokes.explanation: Two words that do enormous work. Dan's 'I know' is simultaneously: (1) the expected response to 'you know?' (2) an absurdist claim of comprehension (3) confirmation that he does know what she's covering up (4) the mutual conspiratorial signal they needed. Maximum economy. The joke is entirely in the gap between the complexity of what Selina said and the simplicity of Dan's answer. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'I'm sorry to hear that you lost... that you're no longer pregnant.'
jokes.setup: Selina apologizes for snapping at Amy; Amy responds with condolences on the miscarriage
jokes.punchline: Amy's fumbled, self-correcting condolence — she can't say 'lost the baby' because that's exactly what happened but it sounds wrong — ending on the bizarrely clinical 'no longer pregnant'
jokes.explanation: Amy's verbal fumbling over how to express condolences for a miscarriage is acutely observed — everyone has struggled with this exact social situation. The mid-sentence self-correction and the landing on 'no longer pregnant' (accurate but inhuman) is good cringe comedy grounded in real awkwardness.

jokes.text: Amy's 'buffering' nod — 'The nod of my head is like I'm buffering.'
jokes.setup: Selina reveals Ted is about to dump her; Amy nods in a way that Selina notices and questions
jokes.punchline: Amy explains the nod is 'like I'm buffering' — using a tech metaphor to describe her brain processing information she can't respond to honestly
jokes.explanation: 'Buffering' as a metaphor for diplomatic information processing is sharp and contemporary. Amy's self-description of her own face managing Selina's revelations is a precise character observation — Amy is constantly managing the gap between what she thinks and what she can say. The tech metaphor is well-chosen and quotable.

jokes.text: 'Okay, I think you'd better get out of my office.' — Selina's response to Amy's unfinished sentence
jokes.setup: Amy has stopped herself mid-sentence, but the implication hangs in the air
jokes.punchline: Selina doesn't ask Amy to finish — she already knows what comes next and ejects her before she has to hear it
jokes.explanation: Selina's pre-emptive ejection of Amy is a perfect reaction beat — she understands exactly what Amy didn't say and acts on the unspoken truth. The comedic and emotional beats work simultaneously here, which is rare and valuable.

jokes.text: 'I know, who'd have thought transparent government would take up so much of your field of vision?'
jokes.setup: Press corps is gathered to receive the disclosed documents
jokes.punchline: Selina's sardonic greeting frames the entire exercise as a visual spectacle — 'field of vision' suggesting the paperwork/screens as a literal obstruction
jokes.explanation: A functional Selina quip that comments on the exercise's absurdity. The irony is appropriate — 'transparent government' is blocking everyone's view. Decent but not a standout line.

jokes.text: Mike claims going to Iraq with the VP to 'present a Thanksgiving turkey' counts as being 'in the line of fire'
jokes.setup: Collins challenges Mike about being 'in the line of fire'; Mike claims he's been to Iraq with the VP
jokes.punchline: The nature of his Iraq service: presenting a Thanksgiving turkey. Not combat. Not even close to a fire line.
jokes.explanation: Mike's credibility bluster punctured by the most peacetime possible form of military adjacency — turkey presentation. The addition 'I was there. No fire as I recall.' from Collins is the killer follow-up. Good character comedy for Mike.

jokes.text: 'No, but the line was there.' — Mike's comeback about the turkey-presenting 'line of fire'
jokes.setup: Collins points out there was no fire in Mike's Iraq turkey-presenting trip
jokes.punchline: Mike insists 'the line was there' — parsing 'line of fire' as merely requiring the existence of a line, not actual fire
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comedic logic. Mike's literal parsing of 'line of fire' — claiming the metaphor is satisfied if just the 'line' element is present (a food line, presumably) — is both wordplay and character comedy. It's Mike's delusional self-regard expressed as linguistic sophistry. 'No, but the line was there' is five words that do everything. Highly quotable, justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'So how's the place been over at Arlington Cemetery going? I hear it's pretty nice there.' — Mike asks Collins about Arlington Cemetery
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to make small talk with Collins, who has been reassigned to Arlington Cemetery
jokes.punchline: 'I hear it's pretty nice there' — commenting on a cemetery like it's a resort; the vacancy of his social skills on full display
jokes.explanation: Mike's complete social obliviousness — treating Arlington Cemetery as a pleasant posting to chitchat about — is pure character comedy. The cheerful 'I hear it's pretty nice there' about a military cemetery is a lovely dark/oblivious blend.

jokes.text: 'Does he ever say anything funny?' — Mike asks Collins about his uncle with dementia
jokes.setup: Collins mentions he's also caring for an uncle with dementia as part of his burdens
jokes.punchline: Mike's response — 'does he ever say anything funny?' — treating a dementia patient as a potential comedy source, utterly unaware of the inappropriateness
jokes.explanation: Mike's social tone-deafness reaching its peak. 'Does he ever say anything funny?' applied to a dementia patient is dark, unexpected, and completely character-specific — Mike's discomfort with serious conversations manifesting as desperately hunting for a joke. The question is asked with genuine curiosity, which makes it worse and funnier. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'No, you were transferred for being jocular and overfamiliar. Security guys are supposed to be like wallpaper. They're not supposed to be grinning like some ape.'
jokes.setup: Collins says he was 'fucking transferred for nothing'
jokes.punchline: Mike defending the transfer by calling Collins an 'ape' — the exact language that has become a PR crisis for the office, which Mike is there to defuse
jokes.explanation: Mike being sent to smooth things over, then immediately calling Collins an 'ape' — the exact slur that has already made the news (as we learn in the next scene) — is a perfect ironic blunder. The joke is structural: Mike is there to de-escalate and immediately escalates. 'Wallpaper' as the ideal secret service metaphor is a nicely observed detail.

jokes.text: The press is calling the disclosure strategy an 'e-nami'
jokes.setup: The Times is saying the disclosure is a 'deluge' strategy
jokes.punchline: Jonah reports the paper is calling it an 'e-nami' — e-tsunami, an email tsunami
jokes.explanation: A decent portmanteau joke — 'e-nami' as e-mail tsunami is clean and efficient. The joke is that Selina's scheme has already been named and satirized by the press, which is a nice escalation of the episode's plot.

jokes.text: 'Is anybody picking up on the back facials? The press was supposed to pick on those damn back facials.'
jokes.setup: The press disclosure has generated stories but not the intended ones
jokes.punchline: Selina is upset that her backne isn't the big story — she sacrificed her dermatological dignity for nothing
jokes.explanation: Good callback that pays off the backne setup. The frustration that the back facials didn't work as planned — Selina having exposed her personal embarrassment for nothing — is a nice escalation of the earlier joke.

jokes.text: The 'Washington Post' disclosure: emails suggesting a birthday gift of 'a cake in the shape of a dick' and 'a smart new hat in the shape of a dick'
jokes.setup: The Post has found an embarrassing email in the disclosure — gift suggestions for a White House aide
jokes.punchline: Suggestion 1: cake shaped like a dick. Suggestion 2: a hat — specifically described as 'smart new' — shaped like a dick. The second item's incongruous formal modifier ('smart new') makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'dick cake' to 'smart new hat in the shape of a dick' is where this joke earns its keep — the second suggestion's incongruous formality ('smart new') applied to a phallic hat is a nice comedic escalation. 'Oh, that made it in there!' as a reaction is also good.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Oh, that made it in there!' — realizing the dick hat email was released
jokes.setup: Mike's email about dick-shaped gifts has been released
jokes.punchline: Jonah's delighted 'Oh, that made it in there!' — confirming he was the intended recipient and had seen the original email; his glee is completely disproportionate
jokes.explanation: Jonah's reaction reveals the backstory (he was the birthday boy) while demonstrating his complete political obliviousness (he's delighted that his humiliating gift suggestions are now public record). Good character beat that extends the previous joke.

jokes.text: 'Everybody knows this is me, guys.' — Jonah
jokes.setup: The dick cake email has been published; Amy says 'everybody knows this is me'
jokes.punchline: Jonah confidently claims the dick cake email — apparently having sent it, and apparently comfortable asserting this as common knowledge
jokes.explanation: Jonah's complete lack of shame about the dick cake email being attributed to him — in fact, his confidence that everyone already knows — is pure character comedy. His obliviousness to why this is bad is the joke.

jokes.text: 'There are 3,000 people on the White House payroll, Jonah.'
jokes.setup: Jonah insists 'everybody' knows the dick cake email is his
jokes.punchline: Amy points out that 3,000 people working there means 'everybody' is not the intimate circle Jonah imagines — the scale deflates his notoriety-pride
jokes.explanation: A good deflation of Jonah's self-importance. The specific number (3,000) grounds the abstract and makes the correction feel real. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'West Wing, Jonah.' / 'No, who is DC?' / '...Uh, Dick Cake.'
jokes.setup: Amy tells Jonah to go to the West Wing; he asks 'who is DC?' as an acronym question
jokes.punchline: Someone (presumably Ryan) reveals DC = Dick Cake
jokes.explanation: The DC = Dick Cake acronym reveal is a tight running gag callback. The economy is excellent — the joke lands on three short lines. Washington DC / Dick Cake is the kind of layered wordplay Veep specializes in.

jokes.text: 'Every time Ted stayed overnight at the residence has been fully disclosed.' — the accidental outing of Selina's relationship
jokes.setup: The disclosure has accidentally revealed Ted's overnight stays
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, Ted's gonna love that.' — immediate recognition that this is a privacy disaster for Selina's relationship on top of everything else
jokes.explanation: A nice ironic twist — the full disclosure backfires in personal as well as political terms. The unintended consequences of the scheme rippling into Selina's private life is good plot-comedy integration.

jokes.text: 'Mike, can't you make me not have been pregnant?'
jokes.setup: Bloggers are linking Ted's overnighters to the pregnancy rumors; Selina is drowning
jokes.punchline: Selina asks Mike to retroactively un-make her pregnant — as if PR spin has the power to rewrite biology
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Can't you make me not have been pregnant?' is a perfect encapsulation of Selina's worldview: that reality is a PR problem that press officers can fix. The absurdist logic is completely character-specific — only someone who has spent their life managing narratives would make this request with apparent sincerity. Grammatically tortured ('not have been') in a way that perfectly mirrors the impossibility being requested. Highly quotable, justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'This is what happens when you tell the truth. Nothing good comes of it.'
jokes.setup: The disclosure has backfired catastrophically
jokes.punchline: Gary's sincere philosophical conclusion that truth-telling is simply a bad strategy — spoken as pure moral advice, not satire
jokes.explanation: Gary's deadpan delivery of an anti-honesty moral lesson as if it's received wisdom is sharp dark comedy and sharp political satire simultaneously. 'Nothing good comes of it' said about truth-telling in a government office is both funny and depressing. Quotable.

jokes.text: 'Okay, I'm gonna get you a cup of rose hip tea.' — Gary's solution to all crises
jokes.setup: The VP's office is in full crisis meltdown
jokes.punchline: Gary's response to institutional chaos: offer tea — his total subordination of real problems to Selina's personal comfort
jokes.explanation: A reliable Gary character beat — his value system has Selina's physical comfort as the highest priority regardless of context. The tea offer in the midst of actual political crisis is a running character joke.

jokes.text: 'This is gonna cause a total potal meltdown.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is warning that POTUS will be unhappy
jokes.punchline: 'Potal' — POTUS + total, meaning a Presidential-scale total meltdown. Jonah's attempt at a portmanteau is slightly mangled.
jokes.explanation: Jonah coining 'potal meltdown' is a decent piece of Washington insider wordplay — the blending of 'POTUS' and 'total' into a crisis descriptor. Slightly clumsy but appropriate for Jonah's character (he tries to be clever and mostly gets there).

jokes.text: 'Yeah, why don't you run along and see if you can suck your own cake?'
jokes.setup: Dan is dismissing Jonah after the crisis meeting
jokes.punchline: 'Suck your own cake' — a riff on the dick cake situation, telling Jonah to go perform an anatomically improbable act on his own infamous baked good
jokes.explanation: A sharp callback that weaponizes the episode's running gag as an insult. 'Suck your own cake' is both a creative dismissal and a precise character humiliation that references Jonah's specific situation. Good economy and quotability.

jokes.text: 'Ace play, Shakespeare. Really. When in doubt, call a vet an ape.'
jokes.setup: Mike has apparently called Collins (a veteran) an 'ape' in the press interaction
jokes.punchline: Amy's sarcastic 'Ace play, Shakespeare' — grading his crisis management skills while summarizing his blunder with devastating economy
jokes.explanation: Amy's 'Ace play, Shakespeare' is a sharp double-insult: sarcastic compliment + elevated literary reference to underscore how stupid the move was. The callback to the ape comment payoffs nicely.

jokes.text: 'Exactly. We are gonna fire someone.' — the group's answer to 'what are we gonna do?'
jokes.setup: Selina asks what they're going to do about the crisis
jokes.punchline: The group's immediate consensus is to fire someone — not fix anything, but sacrifice a person
jokes.explanation: A sharp political satire beat — the office's problem-solving instinct is purely sacrificial rather than corrective. 'Does anybody have a better idea?' underlines that this is the consensus default, not a desperate last resort.

jokes.text: Selina about who to fire: 'Sue.' Others: 'Oh, Sue.' Selina: 'That's funny, you guys. But seriously, Sue's not important enough.'
jokes.setup: The team is trying to identify who to fire as the fall guy
jokes.punchline: Everyone agrees on Sue as if it's obvious — then Selina dismisses it not because Sue is valuable but because she's not important enough to make the firing newsworthy
jokes.explanation: Sue's invisibility turned into a specific kind of institutional powerlessness — she can't even be fired for PR value. The team's immediate 'Oh, Sue' consensus is a perfect group comedy beat.

jokes.text: 'Suck-up isn't gonna fix a fuck-up, Dan.'
jokes.setup: Dan tries to blame his colleagues while distancing himself from responsibility
jokes.punchline: Selina's rhyming rebuke — 'suck-up isn't gonna fix a fuck-up' — cutting and economical
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect Veep one-liner. The rhyme is tight ('suck-up' / 'fuck-up'), the meaning is precise, the character application is perfect (Dan is exactly this kind of political animal), and it's completely Selina in tone — no nonsense, rhythmically satisfying, vulgar but not gratuitously so. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Shut?' / 'Of course shut!' — the door interaction
jokes.setup: Everyone leaves the office; presumably Gary lingers at the door
jokes.punchline: Gary asks if he should shut the door; Selina's exasperated 'of course shut!' — her total control over even this smallest decision
jokes.explanation: A small character beat that captures Gary's constant need to check with Selina on everything. Brief but character-consistent.

jokes.text: The rose hip tea with honey and Fig Newton ceremony
jokes.setup: Selina is in her office dealing with a cascade of crises
jokes.punchline: Gary presents the tea with honey (as specified) plus an unsolicited Fig Newton — the infantilizing attention to detail of her snack presentation
jokes.explanation: The Fig Newton addition — Gary going beyond his brief to add a cookie, the VP accepting it with 'outstanding' — is a portrait of their codependent relationship. 'Outstanding, Gary' about a cookie in the middle of a political storm is a nice tonal juxtaposition.

jokes.text: Ted's reaction to press attention on his overnights: 'I thought I was fucking you in private.'
jokes.setup: Ted is angry that his relationship with Selina has been disclosed
jokes.punchline: 'I thought I was fucking you in private' — his complaint is accurate but phrased with a complete lack of romantic euphemism
jokes.explanation: Ted's blunt complaint cuts through any romantic framing of their relationship. The joke is in the phrasing — 'fucking you in private' as a relationship expectation rather than a desire. Characteristically Veep in its refusal of romantic language.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I love to fuck you in private.' — her attempt to calm Ted
jokes.setup: Ted says he thought he was being fucked in private; Selina agrees she loves this
jokes.punchline: Her validation of his complaint becomes accidentally farcical — 'I love to fuck you in private' said as reassurance in a completely unromantic context
jokes.explanation: Selina's attempt at reassurance landing as a clinical declaration is a nice absurdist beat. The scene's cringe escalation continues effectively.

jokes.text: 'Hey, you know what? Thanks for returning my call. The message was, "please don't fucking call me again."'
jokes.setup: A journalist apparently calls in the middle of the argument
jokes.punchline: Ted's response to the journalist — quoting the previous message as a reminder — then escalating to 'take your beak and shove it up some corpse's ass, okay, you vulture motherfucker?'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from professional callback-reminder to 'shove it up some corpse's ass, you vulture motherfucker' is excellent. The extended metaphor (beak, corpse, vulture) is vivid and sustained. The 'corpse' detail — specifying the ass being a dead person's — is an absurdist escalation that elevates this above a generic insult.

jokes.text: 'Was that a journalist?' / 'Yes.' / 'Oh, God!' / 'What are you doing, Ted? You can't tell him to fuck his mother.' / 'Her mother, actually.'
jokes.setup: Selina is horrified that Ted has told a journalist to shove it up a corpse's ass
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'you can't tell him to fuck his mother' is corrected by Ted: it was a woman journalist — 'her mother, actually' — Ted defending his specific accuracy while being completely wrong about everything else
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent — Selina's actual crisis (Ted insulted a journalist) is immediately displaced by a gender-specificity correction ('her mother, actually') that Ted delivers with total calm. The absurdity of correcting the gender of the hypothetical mother while having just committed a PR catastrophe is a perfect comic non-sequitur that feels completely believable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Selina asking Gary to break up with Ted for her — 'I want you to let him go'
jokes.setup: Selina needs to end the relationship but doesn't want to do it herself
jokes.punchline: She delegates the breakup to Gary — as if it's an administrative task — and Gary has to process this assignment
jokes.explanation: Selina treating her romantic breakup as an administrative delegation to Gary is a perfect character moment — it reveals both her avoidance of personal consequences and her total instrumentalization of Gary. Gary's barely suppressed 'wouldn't it be better if it came from you?' is the appropriate reaction.

jokes.text: 'Because I think that he is about to dump me. So I'm not gonna be the dumpee. I'm gonna be the dumper. Okay? He's gonna be the dumpee.'
jokes.setup: Gary asks why Selina can't break up with Ted herself
jokes.punchline: Selina's elaborate 'dumper/dumpee' logic — she can't risk being dumped so she's pre-emptively dumping via proxy — the childish vanity expressed in political terms
jokes.explanation: The dumper/dumpee taxonomy applied with Selina's full political self-interest is sharp character comedy. The repetition of 'dumpee' — clarifying three times who the dumpee is — escalates nicely. The VP of the United States explaining relationship power dynamics like a middle schooler is the joke.

jokes.text: Gary telling Ted it's over: 'She can go off, can't she?' / 'Yeah, sorry about that.' / 'That's okay. So maybe it's a good thing.' / 'What? What's a good thing?' / 'That she doesn't want to see you anymore.'
jokes.setup: Gary arrives at Ted's house to break up with Selina on her behalf; there's small talk about Selina's temper
jokes.punchline: Gary's transition from pleasantries to 'that she doesn't want to see you anymore' — the abruptness of the actual message after the 'nice house' courtesy
jokes.explanation: Gary's proxy-breakup conversation is perfectly handled. The small talk about Selina going off, the awkward transition, the moment of confusion before the actual message lands — it's cringe comedy with good structural timing. 'Your house is so nice. Oh, thank you' as preamble to being dumped by a third party is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: Ted's long stunned silence after being told Selina doesn't want to see him — 16 seconds of dead air before Gary speaks
jokes.setup: Gary has just told Ted that Selina doesn't want to see him anymore
jokes.punchline: An extended silence — Ted processing being dumped by his girlfriend's bag carrier
jokes.explanation: Non-verbal comedy is often the hardest to execute. This silence is working on multiple levels simultaneously: Gary's awkwardness at having just delivered a breakup, Ted's humiliation at being dumped by a proxy, the absurdity of the whole situation. The silence IS the joke — it's the sound of a man processing the most undignified romantic rejection possible. Economy score 10/10 because it uses zero words. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Oh, ma'am. You've been off radar for over an hour. Oh, did I miss anything? Did the president ride through on a lion or something?'
jokes.setup: A staffer notes Selina has been off radar
jokes.punchline: 'Did the president ride through on a lion or something?' — Selina's hyperbolic benchmark for what would justify a missed call
jokes.explanation: A fun Selina absurdist image — the President on a lion as the threshold for urgency. The specificity of the lion (not just 'horse' or 'elephant') is the right comedic choice. Light and clean.

jokes.text: 'All right. I don't want to end up with a horse's head in my bed. Though there's room for one now.'
jokes.setup: Selina is told she needs to drop in on the gaming association
jokes.punchline: Godfather reference + immediate self-deprecating addition: 'though there's room for one now' — acknowledging Ted has just left/been dumped
jokes.explanation: The Godfather reference is standard, but the pivot — 'though there's room for one now' — elevates it dramatically. Selina making a darkly comic quip about the absence of her recently-dumped boyfriend within seconds of him leaving is character-perfect. The joke acknowledges real emotional loss through deflective humor, which is quintessential Selina. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Gary using a farm animal fable to tell Dan and Amy who's getting fired: horse, sheep, pig — 'Who does she get rid of?'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy want to know who's being fired; Gary says he won't tell them anything
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately offers a thinly veiled fable — 'let's pretend there's a female farmer...' — as if this is meaningfully different from just telling them
jokes.explanation: Gary's complete failure to understand that a parable with an obvious solution isn't actually keeping a secret is a nice character beat. His loyalty to Selina combined with his inability to resist being helpful creates this funny contradiction.

jokes.text: Gary: 'What? What? Did you just call me buddy? You don't listen to me. You never listen to me. What's going on? You want something from me.'
jokes.setup: Someone (Dan or Amy) has called Gary 'buddy' while fishing for information about who's being fired.
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately recognizes the manipulation — being called 'buddy' has never happened, so it's transparently fake — and calls it out with wounded accuracy.
jokes.explanation: Gary's self-awareness here is a nice break from his usual obliviousness. 'You want something from me' is accurate and a bit sad. His radar for being used is sharp even if his radar for everything else is dim.

jokes.text: 'I'd rather be a pig than an ass-monkey up on his high horse. There, that's three animals for you.'
jokes.setup: Gary has answered 'the pig' to his own fable question, then realizes he's given them information
jokes.punchline: He tries to recover with more animal metaphors, mixing idioms, then counts 'that's three animals for you' as if the number itself neutralizes what he said
jokes.explanation: 'Ass-monkey up on his high horse' is a good mixed-metaphor insult, and Gary counting 'that's three animals for you' as a deflection is funny in its logic-failure. The whole sequence of Gary accidentally revealing everything while trying not to is well-structured.

jokes.text: The suicide pact proposal: Dan proposes they all threaten to resign together so Selina can't fire any of them
jokes.setup: Dan wants protection against being fired
jokes.punchline: His solution is a 'suicide pact' — a mutually assured destruction of employment that he frames with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: The 'suicide pact' framing for a mutual resignation threat is a nice dark-absurdist coinage. Dan's political cynicism expressed as a genuine strategic proposal. The logic is actually sound (she can't fire all of them without a crisis) which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'One condition... I watch you die first and I get to stab you repeatedly to make sure.'
jokes.setup: Amy agrees to the suicide pact with conditions
jokes.punchline: Her condition: she gets to watch Dan die first and repeatedly stab him — maintaining the suicide metaphor while expressing her genuine desire to harm Dan
jokes.explanation: A perfect escalation of the suicide pact metaphor. Amy's condition — she gets to stab Dan repeatedly to confirm death — is funny because it simultaneously plays the metaphor straight and reveals her genuine hostility to Dan. 'Repeatedly to make sure' is the killer addition. Very Amy, very quotable.

jokes.text: 'Why would we let you in on our suicide pact?' / 'Because three is better than two.' / 'Not with testicles.'
jokes.setup: Mike wants to join the suicide pact; Dan and Amy question why
jokes.punchline: Mike says 'three is better than two'; Amy's comeback: 'Not with testicles' — implying adding Mike (a testicle metaphor) degrades rather than improves the pact
jokes.explanation: Amy's 'Not with testicles' is one of the episode's best one-liners. The comeback works on two levels: (1) the obvious anatomical reference to Mike, (2) the implicit argument that testicles impede rather than help (hanging, dragging things down). Three words, maximum insult, perfect economy. The structure is textbook — Mike's reasonable-sounding 'three is better than two' is set up perfectly for the subversion. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Mike reveals he was part of a suicide pact in the '90s with Congressman Hartigan. Dan: 'What happened?' Mike: 'Oh, we got fired.'
jokes.setup: Mike presents his experience with a '90s suicide pact as evidence he should be admitted.
jokes.punchline: The pact failed immediately and everyone got fired — making his reference experience an anti-recommendation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mike joke structure. He presents his experience as authority; the reveal is that his experience is proof of failure. 'Oh, we got fired' delivered as if it's a neutral fact rather than disqualifying evidence is completely Mike. The brevity and timing are excellent. Well above average — character-integrated, economical, perfectly structured.

jokes.text: Jonah reveals an email from Dan to Macauley's chief of staff: 'the senator says, "thanks, buddy"'
jokes.setup: Jonah has been unredacting documents and claims to have found incriminating evidence against Dan
jokes.punchline: The 'incriminating' email is just 'the senator says thanks, buddy' — so mild that Dan correctly identifies it as 'hardly incriminating'
jokes.explanation: Jonah's big reveal being 'thanks, buddy' is a good deflation of his dramatic buildup. But the line 'there's probably more where that came from' correctly implies the danger is real, which gives this a plot-comedy double function.

jokes.text: Mike about unredacting: 'I'm getting really good at dacting.' Dan: 'Do you mean un-redacting?' Mike: 'Taking the black off.'
jokes.setup: Mike is removing redactions from documents
jokes.punchline: Mike has coined 'dacting' for un-redacting — eliminating the prefix and creating a new, wronger word; 'taking the black off' as a clarification is somehow worse
jokes.explanation: 'Dacting' is a wonderful Mike coinage — he's eliminated the 'un-re' prefix and produced a new word that sounds wrong but follows a logic. 'Taking the black off' as an explanation is the right Mike clarification: literally accurate, completely unhelpful. This is Mike at his most specifically Mike.

tmdb_overview: The vice president's office takes some heat over a pregnancy rumor and the firing of a Secret Service agent, so Selina establishes some new ground rules about internal correspondence. Meanwhile, Dan tries to cover his boss' tracks on the clean-jobs bill; and a staffer's job may be in jeopardy.
insight: Escalation-driven finale hits 71 jokes in 31 minutes but falls short of elite tier.
